title: Avgolemono soup
subtitle: Greek chicken, rice, and egg-lemon soup
excerpt: A Greek soup of chicken stock and rice thickened at the last moment with beaten egg and lemon juice, giving it a silky, slightly tart finish unlike any other broth. Simple to make, impossible to replicate with shortcuts. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Avgolemono is one of the foundational preparations of Greek cooking, documented by Claudia Roden in The Food of the Eastern Mediterranean and by Diane Kochilas in The Greek Vegetarian. The egg-lemon thickening technique (avgo = egg, lemono = lemon) appears across Greek soups, stews, and sauce preparations.

content.text: Avgolemono is the technique and the soup at the same time. The egg-lemon mixture whisked into hot chicken stock thickens it into something between a broth and a light custard, silky and sharp without any cream. It is the kind of soup that reads as complicated until you make it, at which point it is clearly one of the simplest things in the Greek kitchen.

The only technical requirement is tempering: you cannot add beaten egg directly to boiling stock or it scrambles. Ladle some of the hot soup into the egg-lemon bowl first, whisking as you go, to bring the eggs up to temperature gradually. Then pour the mixture back into the pot. Off the heat. The residual warmth does the rest.

items.name: Chicken breast
items.prepNote: cooked and shredded, or use leftover roast chicken

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: white pepper is traditional if you have it

content.text: The quality of the stock determines the soup. A good homemade stock from a whole chicken, boiled with onion, celery, and bay then strained, gives the best result. Good shop-bought stock works too; avoid very salty versions.

Bring {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock to a simmer in a large saucepan. Add {{long-grain-rice}} of rice and cook for 18 to 20 minutes until the rice is tender. If adding shredded chicken, stir it in for the last 5 minutes to warm through. Reduce the heat to very low.

In a bowl, whisk {{eggs}} eggs with the juice of {{lemon}} lemons until combined. Ladle about 4 spoonfuls of the hot (but not boiling) stock slowly into the egg mixture, whisking constantly as you pour. This is the
step. Pour the tempered mixture back into the pot and stir gently. Do not boil the soup again, the eggs will curdle. Season with salt and pepper, scatter with parsley, and serve immediately.

items.fix: Remove the pot from the heat before adding the tempered egg mixture. Never boil the soup after this stage.
items.cause: Stock was boiling when the egg mixture was added, or the soup was returned to the boil after adding the eggs.
items.symptom: Soup curdled with visible egg strands

items.fix: The soup thickens as it cools slightly. Serve promptly rather than holding on the hob. For a thicker version, use 100 g of rice.
items.cause: Not enough rice, or the eggs were not properly beaten before tempering.
items.symptom: Too thin after the egg-lemon goes in

items.fix: Start with the juice of 1.5 lemons and taste before adding more. The acidity should be present but not aggressive.
items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or lemon added before the eggs had fully integrated.

items.fix: Simmer the stock for 10 minutes with a piece of celery and a bay leaf before proceeding. Taste and dilute with water if too salty.

content.text: Avgolemono turns up across the Eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East. It thickens sauces for stuffed vine leaves in Lebanon. It loosens Greek stews. As soup, it belongs to Greece. The egg-and-lemon mix runs centuries older than cream-based French sauces in the region. It gives a richness and a sharp acidity cream can come close to but not match. Greeks make it when someone is ill, when it is cold, or when there is leftover chicken in the fridge. It covers most of the times you want something warm and steadying.
